lesson.menu_title: The Habeas Corpus — Challenging Unlawful Detention
lesson.menu_desc: Causative (have / get something done) for demanding the writ and freeing the client -- 29 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 16 -- The Habeas Corpus: challenging an arbitrary detention and demanding the citizen be produced or released.
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Luiz Bressane | Aula 16 -- The Habeas Corpus

listenings.text: Habeas corpus is the oldest safeguard a citizen has, and the fastest weapon in your hands as a defender. It does not wait for a trial. The words mean: produce the body. When you file it, you force the State to bring your client before a judge and to justify the detention on the spot, or release the person at once. That is the whole power of the writ. So do not argue guilt or innocence here; argue liberty. Liberty cannot be removed without a lawful order, and no one may be held arbitrarily, with no warrant, no charge and no hearing. If your client is held incommunicado, you have him produced; if the State cannot justify it, you get him released. Move quickly, because every hour of an unlawful detention is an hour of injustice.

You are listening to the legal affairs review. The High Court today granted a petition for habeas corpus in the case of the detainee Andrade. The petitioner had argued that the detention was arbitrary and unlawful: the client had been held for six days with no warrant, no charge and no hearing, and kept incommunicado without access to a defender. The State asked the court to accept that an ongoing investigation was a sufficient basis to hold him. The court disagreed, recognising the writ as the citizen's first safeguard. It ordered the State to justify the detention by noon or have the detainee released, and directed that the custodian produce him before the bench. We understand the client was freed the same afternoon.
